coredata.dc:title: Rational design of high quality citric acid-derived carbon dots by selecting efficient chemical structure motifs
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Fang
Ma, Shiyao
Li, Jun
Dai, Kang
Xiao, Xincai
Zhao, Dan
Gong, Weifan
coredata.dc:description: A green pyrolysis approach to prepare ultra-small nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-C-dots, QY reaches 55.7%) with precursors citric acid (CA) as carbon source and N-acetyl-l-cysteine as N and S dopant has been explored. The optical properties and the origin of photoluminescence (PL) have been investigated at length. The mixed excitation independence character (200 ≤ λ ex ≤ 380 nm) and the excitation dependence feature (λ ex > 380 nm) along with the biexponential behavior of lifetime of prepared C-dots all indicate the existence of two different emissive sites: the surface state caused by the introducing of N and S on C-dots surfaces and the molecular state induced by the fluorophores covalently bonded onto C-dots surfaces. More importantly, for the first time, through comprehensively comparing the PL performances of various types of N,S-C-dots, N-C-dots and S-C-dots synthesized under same condition, we report the efficient chemical structure motif of reactant, which could form small fluorophore molecules with high PL during the synthesis and thus exhibits favorable impact on PL performances of resulting C-dots. Detailed structural and property studies suggest that the chemical structure motif of reactant (HNCCSH) determines the optical properties of CA-derived C-dots, which would be valuable in selecting efficient reactants for preparing highly fluorescent CA-derived C-dots.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of the molar ratio of L-NAC to CA
Influence of the structure of reactants
The formation pathway of CL-NAC+CA dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province
Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: South-central University for Nationalities Graduate Student Outstanding Thesis Cultivation Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2016CFB615 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the “Central Universities”, South-central University for Nationalities ( CZY15020 , CZW15017 ) and South-central University for Nationalities Graduate Student Outstanding Thesis Cultivation Project ( 2015xwlwpy007 ). Appendices The appendices consist of the PL spectra and UV–vis spectra of C L-NAC+CA dots prepared at 70 °C for various reaction times (3, 6, 9, and 12 h) without carbonalization, PL spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra of a series of S-C-dots, N-C-dots and N,S-C-dots, PL spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra of C L-Cys+CA dots and C L-NAC+CA dots (10 mg mL −1 ) prepared at 70 °C for 12 h without carbonalization, the proposed structure of polymer cluster, fluorescence chromatogram of the product 3 generated by L-NAC and CA in the carbonization stage, TEM image of the C L-NAC+CA dots, Zeta potential changes in the different pH, photographs of C L-NAC+CA dots dissolved in various types of organic solvents under UV light of 365 nm, the excitation-independent PL behaviors of C L-NAC+CA dots prepared at 70 °C for 12 h without carbonalization, and fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL spectra, (b) UV–visible absorption spectra and (c) the QYs of CL-NAC+CA dots prepared at 70 °C for various reaction times (3, 6, 9, and 12 h) (λ ex = 340 nm); (d) PL spectra, (e) UV–visible absorption spectra and (f) the QYs of CL-NAC+CA dots prepared at 200 °C for various reaction times (2, 3, 4, and 5 h) (λ ex = 340 nm).
(a) PL spectra and (b) UV–visible absorption spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots with different L-NAC/CA values (λ ex = 340 nm); the inset shows the QYs of these prepared C-dots.

Absorption chromatograms of (a) the product 1 generated by L-Cys and CA in the polymerization stage and reagent L-Cys, and (b) the product 2 generated by L-NAC and CA in the polymerization stage and reagent L-NAC monitored at 260 nm. The insets displays the corresponding fluorescence chromatograms of product 1 and product 2 monitored at excitation/emission wavelengths of 358/450 nm. Chromatograms are offset for clarity.
IR spectra of free CA, L-NAC and CL-NAC+CA dots. Spectra are offset for clarity.
XPS spectra recorded from CL-NAC+CA dots: (a) Overall picture, (b) C (1s), (c) N (1s) and (d) S (2p).

Stability of CL-NAC+CA dots. (a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CL-NAC+CA dots. (b) Effect of ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensity of CL-NAC+CA dots (ionic strengths were controlled by various concentrations of NaCl in aqueous solution). (c) Photostability of CL-NAC+CA dots. (d) Effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of CL-NAC+CA dots.

UV–vis absorption spectrum (left), excitation spectrum (middle) and emission spectrum (right) of CL-NAC+CA dots. Inset: Photograph of CL-NAC+CA dots under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right).
3D PL spectra under the excitation wavelengths range from 200 to 460 nm with 10 nm increments and the emission wavelengths range from 350 to 600 nm with 10 nm increments.

Fluorescence decay curves of (a) CL-NAC+CA dots with different n(L-NAC)/5(CA) values (1, 4, 12), and (b) C-dots prepared with different materials (3-MPA, Met, L-Iso, L-NAC) recorded at room temperature in aqueous solution.
The relationships between general structures and the PL properties of the resultant C-dots.
Proposed formation pathway of CL-NAC+CA dots.
The chemical and structural formulas of precursors and QYs of resulting C-dots.
Fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve.

head.ce:title: Rational design of high quality citric acid-derived carbon dots by selecting efficient chemical structure motifs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green pyrolysis approach to prepare ultra-small nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N,S-C-dots, QY reaches 55.7%) with precursors citric acid (CA) as carbon source and N-acetyl-l-cysteine as N and S dopant has been explored. The optical properties and the origin of photoluminescence (PL) have been investigated at length. The mixed excitation independence character (200 ≤ λ ex ≤ 380 nm) and the excitation dependence feature (λ ex > 380 nm) along with the biexponential behavior of lifetime of prepared C-dots all indicate the existence of two different emissive sites: the surface state caused by the introducing of N and S on C-dots surfaces and the molecular state induced by the fluorophores covalently bonded onto C-dots surfaces. More importantly, for the first time, through comprehensively comparing the PL performances of various types of N,S-C-dots, N-C-dots and S-C-dots synthesized under same condition, we report the efficient chemical structure motif of reactant, which could form small fluorophore molecules with high PL during the synthesis and thus exhibits favorable impact on PL performances of resulting C-dots. Detailed structural and property studies suggest that the chemical structure motif of reactant (HNCCSH) determines the optical properties of CA-derived C-dots, which would be valuable in selecting efficient reactants for preparing highly fluorescent CA-derived C-dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots), as a new type of ultra-small photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterial (<10 nm), have drawn tremendous attention due to their outstanding optical properties, customizable surface functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness, superior resistance to photobleaching, simple and inexpensive preparation, abundance of raw material in nature, etc [1]. After their serendipitous discovery dating back to 2004 [2], C-dots have exhibited great potential as a substitute for toxic metal-based quantum dots (QDs), and as promising candidate for applications of broad ranges, such as drug delivery [3], medical diagnosis [4], two-photon fluorescence imaging [5], fluorescent ink [6], detection of metal ions [7] and catalysis [8].

In the past decade, a number of approaches, with a large variety of techniques and starting materials utilized, have been developed to prepare satisfactory fluorescent C-dots. In general, these synthetic methods can be divided into two types: top-down approaches and bottom-up approaches. The top-down approaches include arc discharge [2], laser ablation [9], chemical oxidation in strong acid [10], or electrochemical synthesis [8], which smash a range of carbon allotropes, such as carbon nanotubes, nanodiamonds or graphite into the products with characteristics of fluorescent nanoparticles. By contrast, in bottom-up approaches, the C-dots are formed from molecular precursors with different chemical functionalities, and the majority of these synthetic routes involve high temperature pyrolysis, by employing mircrowaves [11] solvothermal technique [12], or simple thermal combustion of the organic precursor molecules [13]. Compared to top-down methods, the bottom-up procedures usually require no further surface passivation and allow easy introduction of heteroatoms into C-dots, which could significantly affect the properties of the resulting C-dots.

However, there have long been two issues restricting the development of bottom-up approaches. One is that though a plethora of methods for generating carbon based nanoparticles were reported, the efficient preparation route for high PL and QYs (quantum yields) C-dots were seldom seen in literature [12,14]. The second is that it is difficult to capture the whole picture of the PL mechanism due to the PL mechanism and the structure of prepared C-dots vary along with the diverse synthesis processes and the types of precursor materials [15], thereby making the rational design of high-quality C-dots under the theoretical guidance still challenging. The easy synthesis method that can produce C-dots with high QYs would be the breakthrough to offer a better opportunity to study its PL mechanism and synthesis pathway [16], which is conducive to propelling the theoretical research, and meets the needs of practical application.

Since previous studies have suggested the doping of nitrogen element into C-dots could dramatically enhance its initial optical properties [12,14], top priority should be given to the controlled synthesis of doped C-dots from selected nitrogen-containing molecular precursors [12,14,17–21]. Generally, the preparation of doped C-dots involves the combination of two types of molecular precursor materials, in which one provides the major carbon framework and another introduces other elements into the structure. In this regards, the most prominent combination has been citric acid (CA) as carbon source, while nitrogen containing molecules as the dopants [12,14,17–20]. For example, Zhu et al. produced high-quality N-C-dots by hydrothermal treatment of CA and ethylenediamine (EDA) [12]. Dong et al. prepared N,S-C-dots with strong blue luminescence by thermal treatment of CA and l-cysteine (L-Cys) [14]. Besides, CA-derived N,B-C-dots [22] and N,P-C-dots [23] were also prepared, while N,S-C-dots were specially studied here due to their attractive QYs and easy synthesis route [14,17–19].

The origins of PL from these CA-derived C-dots have not hitherto been fully understood, regardless of several principles have been raised to explain their PL emission origins. For example, Dong et al. argued that the PL emission of N,S-C-dots mainly result from the newly formed surface state (N-state), while sulfur atoms plays a cooperative role by comparing the PL performances of O-C-dots, N-C-dots and N,S-C-dots [14]. Kwon et al. chemically modified the surface of N-C-dots with a series of para-substituted anilines, which featured the same amine head group but had different functional groups including OCH3, −SCH3 and N(CH3)2 to control their PL [24]. Their results proved that there existed at least two competitive PL centers, while surface functional groups determine the wavelength of PL. Zhu et al. [12] and Krysmann et al. [25] have compared PL features of CA-derived C-dots synthesized at different temperatures with EDA or ethanolamine (EA) as N-precursor. They proposed that molecular fluorophores predominate in the PL at low temperatures (<180 °C), while the carbogenic core starts forming at higher temperatures, which plays an ever greater role in contributing to the PL of C-dots as synthesis temperature increases. Zhai et al. demonstrated that the N-doping and amine passivation contribute to the fluorescence enhancement through comparing the PL performances of various types of N-C-dots fabricated by the microwave pyrolysis of CA and various amine molecules, including EDA, diethylamine (DEA), triethylamine (TEA) and 1,4-butanediamine (BDA) [26]. In their study, the PL QY and lifetime increase with increasing N content. Wang et al. synthesized a series of N-C-dots through condensation carbonization of CA and linear polyethylenic amine (PEA) analogues with increased amount of nitrogen and chain length of the precursors. While the longer chain of the PEA molecule gives better photo-stability, but lower PL QYs. They speculated that the PL emission of high QY C-dots origins not only from the sp2/sp3 carbon core and surface passivation of C-dots, but also possibly from the molecular fluorophores integrated in the C-dots, and the cyclic imines that enhanced the conjugated π-domains in the carbon core have favorable impact on PL QY [20].

Therefore, the type of doping elements, the functional groups of reactants, reaction temperature, the contents of nitrogen, and the chain length of precursors all affect the optical property and PL mechanism of prepared C-dots. It is worth noting that several groups have reported the findings of fluorescent synthetic intermediate during the formation of CA derived C-dots, and the small fluorophore molecule that bonded onto the surface of C-dots after carbonization was a major source of C-dots’ fluorescence [15,27,28]. Besides, the raw materials utilized in bottom-up synthesis with CA as the carbon source are mostly small molecule compounds, whose structures' impacts upon the optical quality of prepared products haven't been sufficiently studied. Thus, a comprehensive comparison among the impacts of different structure motifs on the fluorescent synthetic intermediate, the formation pathway and the PL mechanism of CA-derived C-dots will be valuable aids in guiding the rational design of highly fluorescent CA-derived C-dots from the theoretical perspective [15].

Herein, we reported the preparation of N,S-C-dots with thiol N-acetyl-l-cysteine (L-NAC) as the N and S dopant. L-NAC works as an ideal raw material since it contains both N and S elements, possesses excellent water-solubility and biocompatibility, and is inexpensive and inodorous. Through preciously controlling the experimental parameters, the N,S-C-dots with a high QY of 55.7% was obtained. The morphology and structure have been carefully characterized, and the PL mechanism has been systematically studied. Besides, through fully investigating the relationship between the chemical structures of reactants and the PL performances of prepared C-dots by comparing various types of N,S-C-dots, N-C-dots and S-C-dots synthesized under same condition, the possible formation pathway of CA-derived C-dots was proposed. Additionally, a chemical structure motif (HNCCSH) was proposed to be an important factor in enhancing the optical properties of C-dots, which provides the theoretical guidance for the rational design of highly fluorescent CA-derived C-dots.

L-NAC (99%), and dl-Homocysteine (DL-Hcy) (95.0%) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. Cysteamine (β-CA) (≥95.0%) was purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Glutathione (Reduced) (GSH) (98.0%), l-Methionine (L-Met) (98.0%), l-Lysine (L-Lys) (99.0%), l-Isoleucine (L-Iso) (≥99%), l-Glutamic acid (L-Glu) (≥99%), l-Histidine (L-His) (≥99%), l-Arginine (L-Arg) (≥98%), and L-Cys (>99.9%) were purchased from Sigma. CA monohydrate (≥99.5%), D-Cys (>98.5%), urea (≥99.0%), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl) (≥99.5%), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) (98%), sublimed sulfur (S) (≥99.5%), thioacetamide (TAA) (≥99.0%), sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate (Na2S2O3) (≥99.0%), hexamethylene, trichloromethane, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran, acetone, ethanol, methyl alcohol, acetonitrile and trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Methyl alcohol for high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was purchased from Tedia Company. All chemicals used were of analytical grade or of the highest purity available. All solutions were prepared using Milli-Q water (Millipore) as the solvent.

The N,S-C-dots were prepared through a thermal treatment with CA as the carbon source and materials contained both nitrogen and sulfur, such as L-NAC, L-Cys, D-Cys, L-Met, DL-Hcy, GSH, β-CA, and TAA as nitrogen and sulfur precursors. In a typical preparation procedure, CA monohydrate (0.1051 g, 0.5 mmol) and L-NAC (0.0653 g, 0.4 mmol) were dissolved in water (4 mL), followed by evaporation at 70 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the resulted colorless solid products were heated in a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, 6 mL ultrapure water was added to dissolve the black solid product with the assistance of ultrasound. The obtained mixture containing light yellow suspended particles and black sick syrup were then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to remove the black carbon precipitates. The supernatant were finally freeze-dried for further analysis. For other N,S-C-dots, they were prepared by using similar procedures except that L-NAC were replaced with L-Cys, D-Cys, L-Met, DL-Hcy, GSH, β-CA, and TAA respectively.

The synthetic procedures of N-C-dots were the same as those for N,S-C-dots, except that nitrogen and sulfur precursors were replaced by nitrogen precursors, such as L-Glu, L-Iso, NH4Cl, L-Lys, urea, L-His, and L-Arg. The subsequent treatments were the same as those for N,S-C-dots.

The synthetic procedures of S-C-dots were the same as those for N,S-C-dots, except that nitrogen and sulfur precursors were replaced by sulfur precursors, such as 3-MPA, S, and Na2S2O3 as sulfur precursors. The subsequent treatments were the same as those for N,S-C-dots.

UV–visible absorption spectra were acquired with a Lambda-35 UV/visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Company) to determine the bandgap absorption of C-dots. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a LS55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer Company). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions. The TEM sample was prepared by dropping C-dots solution with a mixture of ultrapure water and ethanol with the volume ratio of 1:1 as the solvents onto a super-thin carbon-coated copper grid (200 meshes) with the excess solvent evaporated. The TEM image was obtained at 310 K magnification with an FEI Tecnai G2 20 s-twin transmission electron microscope. XPS measurements were acquired with a VG Multilab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corporation). Zeta-potential measurement was carried out on a Zetasizer nanoseries ZEN3690 (Malvern). Fourier transform infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific), and the sample was tested as solid powder. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with an FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh). Chromatographic separation was conducted on a 4.6 × 250-mm Syncronis C18 5-μm column (Thermo scientific, USA) using an Ultimate-3000 HPLC system (Dionex, USA) equipped with an Ultimate-3000 diode array detector (DAD) and an Ultimate-3000 fluorescence detector. The absorption chromatograms were acquired by monitoring the DAD detector at wavelengths of 260 nm. The fluorescence chromatograms were acquired by monitoring the fluorescence detector at excitation/emission wavelengths of 358/450 nm. The mobile phases were MeOH (A) and 0.1% TFA (B). The gradient started with 10% (A) increased to 90% (A) over 40 min. The flow rate was set at 1.0 mL min−1, the injection volume was 10 μL and the column temperature was maintained at 30 °C.

The QYs of C-dots were calculated by comparing the integrated PL intensities and absorbance values of the samples (exited at 320 nm), using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 aqueous solution (refractive index (η) of 1.33) as the standard (QY = 54%) [29]. All samples dissolved in water (η = 1.33) had absorbance less than 0.1 at 320 nm. The relative QY can be calculated using the below equation:
ΦX = ΦST (GradX/GradST) (ηX 2/ηST 2)
where Φ is the QY, Grad is the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance, and η is the refractive index of the solvent; ST denotes the standard and X denotes the sample.

Since the synthesis of CL-NAC+CA dots involves the thermal treatment of carbon source (CA) and surface modifier (L-NAC) mixture at a lower temperature (70 °C) and the carbonization of the formed polymer cluster at high temperature (200 °C), the effect of reaction time on these two independent processes were investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the PL intensities of prepared C-dots increase gradually along with the reaction time at 70 °C, while the featured peak at 345 nm in the corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectra became more distinct (Fig. 1b). Since the highest QY occurs at 12 h, it is thus used in the following researches. To have a better understanding of the role of the polymerization in the whole synthesis process, the optical properties of the product before the carbonization stage were also investigated. As shown in Fig. A.1b, a featured absorption peak at 260 nm increases gradually along with the reaction time, suggesting the increasingly appearance of CC bonds or carbonyl in this process [30].

By fixing the reaction time at 12 h in the initial stage, impact of reaction time on the carbonization process was studied. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CL-NAC+CA dots synthesized at different times have not changed much, indicating that the reaction time does not affect the structure of CL-NAC+CA dots significantly. When reacting at 200 °C for 3 h, the QY reached the highest 55.7% (Fig. 1f). Thereby, the optimal reaction times were found out, that is, 12 h at 70 °C and 3 h at 200 °C, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the molar ratio of L-NAC to CA
ce:section.ce:para: Since the doping of nitrogen atom and sulfur atom in the C-dots could introduce new surface state, and thus enhances the PL performance [14], the molar ratio of L-NAC to CA was purposely adjusted to examine its influence on the fluorescence performance of prepared C-dots. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 b, without L-NAC (L-NAC:CA = 0:5), no absorption peak appears either at 345 nm or 239 nm and CCA dots shows extremely low PL intensity and QY (1.6%). When augmenting the ratio of L-NAC/CA from 0:5 to 4:5, the PL intensity dramatically enhances and the featured UV–Vis absorption peak at around 345 nm gradually increases and becomes more distinct. When the ratio of L-NAC/CA reaches 4:5, the absorption peak at 239 nm appears and the QY reaches the highest (55.7%). Further increasing the ratio of L-NAC/CA, the UV–Vis absorption spectra as well as the QY shows little change. The peak at 239 nm ascribes to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the other peak centered at 345 nm ascribes to the n-π* transition. While the n-π* transition is related to both doping and surface functionalization at the edge of carbon core, resulting in much enhanced emission [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the structure of reactants
ce:section.ce:para: With CA as the carbon source and another reactant introduces other elements into the structure, a series of doped C-dots were prepared to investigate the impact of reactants' structure onto the optical properties of the products. Firstly, since the reactants and CA both contain C element, the direct source of carbon framework is worthy of discussion. The results show that without CA, the prepared CL-NAC dots possesses a low QY at only 2.4%, suggesting that CA not only provides the carbon source, but more importantly, offered the basic scaffold during the synthesis. Secondly, as mentioned above, without L-NAC, the CCA dots also shows extremely low QY (1.6%), indicating the identical importance of the other reactant (dopant). Subsequently, the impacts of reactants on the optical properties of CA derived C-dots are investigated from following multi-level aspects: the elementary composition, the N content, the existing forms of N and S elements and the chemical structure motif of reactants.

Since the elementary composition determines the types of doped C-dots and thus significantly affects their optical properties, a series of C-dots were prepared, including S-C-dots with 3-MPA, S, and Na2S2O3 as sulfur precursors, a range of N-C-dots with L-Glu, L-Iso, NH4Cl, L-Lys, urea, L-His, and L-Arg as nitrogen precursors, and a number of N,S-C-dots with L-NAC, L-Cys, D-Cys, L-Met, DL-Hcy, GSH, β-CA, and TAA as nitrogen and sulfur precursors. As displayed in Figs. A.2–A.4, different from those of N,S-C-dots, the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-C-dots exhibit very weak absorption peak at 345 nm, and S-C-dots show no absorption peak at 345 nm. While the average QYs of prepared C-dots were N,S-C-dots > N-C-dots > CCA dots > S-C-dots, suggesting that the type of doping element can greatly affect the optical properties of CA-derived C-dots. Scheme 1 correlates the general structures to the PL properties of the resultant C-dots. Next, due to their high QYs, the N,S co-doped C-dots were further studied. Whilst, as illustrated in Fig. A.4, not all N,S-C-dots possess high QYs. The QYs of C3-MPA+CA dots and CTAA+CA dots were less than 10%, which is indicative of the fact that the simple introduction of N and S elements into the carbon framework cannot ensure the high-quality of prepared C-dots.

To find out the deciding factors, we will prioritize the influence of N content owing to its favorable effect [12]. As displayed in Table 1 , the chosen nitrogen and sulfur precursors, except for GSH, possess only one atom of nitrogen and sulfur in each molecular formula. However, the QY (34.8%) of CGSH+CA dots is not the highest, indicating the irrelevant relation of N content to PL QY. Meanwhile, the same conclusion can be reached by comparing the relationship between the N content and the optical properties of N-C-dots. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. A.3, the QYs of prepared N-C-dots exhibit irregular changes with the increasing of N content. This further evidenced the N content is not the key factor that determines the quality of CA-derived C-dots.

Furthermore, the impacts of the existing form of N and S elements were examined. As can be seen from Table 1, the N element mainly exists in forms of NH2 and RNH, which exhibits little impact on the quality of CA-derived C-dots. Whilst, the S element exists in forms of SH, SR and CS, and only SH is beneficial to prepare high-quality C-dots (22.7%). Compared with SH, the SR can greatly decrease the C-dots’ QYs (3.1%). The difference in the existence form of S element resulted in 19.6% difference of the resulting QYs, suggesting the necessary participation of SH. This is because SH is more reactive than SR. In addition, CS is also disadvantageous to prepare high-quality C-dots, owning to the low QYs (6.5%) of CTAA+CA dots.

Does this means the co-existence of RNH and SH in reactants is beneficial to the synthesis of high-quality CA-derived C-dots? Further analysis was carried out to examine the chemical structure of the reactants which could produce N,S-C-dots with high QYs, including CL-NAC+CA, Cβ-CA+CA, CD-Cys+CA, CL-Cys+CA and CGSH+CA dots. It is obvious that these N,S-precursors possess a common chemical structure motif HNCCSH (Table 1). Consequently, we speculated that HNCCSH may play an essential role in the synthesis of C-dots. By comparing the QYs of CDL-Hcy+CA dots (22.7%) and CL-Cys+CA dots (49.8%), it was found that RHN–CCSH is more suitable for fabricating high-quality CA-derived C-dots than RHN–CCCSH. We thus proposed that the chemical structural motif RHN–CCSH could form fluorescent synthetic intermediate through cyclization, dehydration and polymerization reaction with CA during the synthesis process, and the small fluorophore molecule that bonded onto the surface of C-dots after carbonization was a major source of C-dots’ fluorescence [15,27,28,32], thereby being conductive to preparing high-quality CA-derived C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation pathway of CL-NAC+CA dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Studying the formation pathway of how the small molecular containing chemical structural motif HNCCSH produces highly fluorescent C-dots with CA is not only beneficial to the study of their PL mechanism but is also conductive to preciously controlling the experimental parameters. In our study, L-NAC and L-Cys both contain chemical structural motif HNCCSH, and they both can fabricate high quality C-dots with CA. Whereas, the chromatographic separation results of the products generated by L-Cys and CA (product 1) and by L-NAC and CA (product 2) in the polymerization stage (70 °C, 12 h) are quite different. While L-Cys can produce highly fluorescent 5-oxo-2,3-dihydro-5H- [1,3]thiazolo [3,2-a]pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPA) [27] with CA, exhibiting a single peak at 14.1 min with an area of 12999174.0 in fluorescence chromatogram (the inset of Fig. 3 ) and displaying strong blue fluorescence (QYs = 54.5%) with obvious absorption peak at 358 nm (Fig. A.5). By comparison, the main synthetic intermediates formed by L-NAC and CA are corresponding to peaks 2, 4 and 5 in absorption chromatograms after excluding the un-reacted reagents, which have obvious UV signals but no PL signals. It is worth noting that some impurities (N,N-diacetyl-L-Cys, N,S-diacetyl-L-Cys, L-Cys, etc.) appear during the storage of L-NAC, and L-NAC thus displays more than one peak [33]. Besides, the product 2 also shows a weak peak at 14.1 min with an area of 488055.6, which may probably caused by the deacetylation of L-NAC and the trace amount of L-Cys could react with CA and form TPA. As shown in Fig. A.5, the product 2 exhibits weak blue fluorescence (QYs = 17.3%) with unapparent absorption peak at 358 nm. Based on the above discoveries, the possible first-stage formation pathway of CL-NAC+CA dots is proposed. As illustrated in Scheme 2 , small molecules are firstly formed at lower temperature. Besides, the polymer clusters (Scheme A.1) are formed by the condensation between the COOH of CA and the SH of L-NAC along with the esterification of CA with L-NAC (Scheme 2). In this stage, either the small molecule or the polymer cluster produces very weak fluorescence. We then further investigated the fluorescent components of the product 3 generated by L-NAC and CA in the initial carbonization stage (200 °C, 0.5 h) by HPLC. It is turned out that the area of the only peak at 14.1 min in fluorescence chromatogram increases to 48976350.6 after being heated at 200 °C for 0.5 h (Fig. A.6). This suggests that the acetyl is fractured at high temperature and –
N ˙
C could further react with COOH to form TPA. We then suppose that when further accelerating the temperature, the acetyl is easy to fracture and –
N ˙
C could further react with COOH to form rigid fluorophores with greater conjugated structure, thereby significantly enhancing the fluorescence intensity. While further increasing the temperature, the carbon cores are subsequently formed by dehydration of the polymer cluster or consumption of fluorophores and small molecules. Since the carbonization process produces diverse products under harsh reaction conditions, the separation and structural identification of which are being studied, no more detailed pathway can be proposed here.

TEM has been extensively utilized as a powerful tool to study the morphology of C-dots from which the size can be identified. Fig. A.7 displays the TEM image of the as-prepared CL-NAC+CA dots. It is quite evident that these C-dots are close to spherical with good dispersion and are uniform with an average diameter of 2.6 nm.

IR measurement was used to investigate the surface functionality of CL-NAC+CA dots. Fig. 4 depicts the IR spectra of CA, L-NAC and the as-prepared CL-NAC+CA dots. The broad absorption peaks attributing to the OH stretching (∼3476 cm−1) and NH stretching (∼3146 cm−1) as well as the relatively sharp absorption peak corresponding to CH (2934 cm−1) are found in the spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots. Different from the CL-NAC+rice dots synthesized by Hu et al. [34], SH stretching (2590 cm−1) band still exists in our CL-NAC+CA dots, indicating the existence of a small part of initial SH groups on the surface of C-dots other than incorporated into the N,S-C-dots. Moreover, the characteristic absorption peaks for CO stretching (1709 cm−1), amido CONH bending (1519 cm−1), amido CON (1398 cm−1) and CS (1208 cm−1) stretching can also be identified by IR spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots, showing the presence of carboxylic, amido and alkyl sulfide functionalities on CL-NAC+CA dots. In addition, a new absorption peak at 1652 cm−1 is observed in the IR spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots, suggesting the formation of CC unsaturated bonds in the carbon cores. The IR spectra testify that at high temperature, N and S elements are efficiently doped into carbon framework of C-dots, which are consistent with XPS analysis.

XPS is a quantitative, surface analytical tool sensitive to the atomic composition of the outermost 100 Å of a sample surface [35]. As revealed by the survey XPS spectrum of CL-NAC+CA dots (Fig. 5 a), the typical product mainly contains C, O, N, and S elements. The appearances of characteristic peaks at binding energies of 284.61, 531.55, 400.07, and 163.54 eV correspond to the elements of C1s, O1s, N1s, and S2p, respectively. The C: O: N: S ratio based on XPS peak are as corrected for element sensitivity was 63.87: 24.82: 5.86: 5.45 for CL-NAC+CA dots, which declares the successful doping of N and S heteroatoms into C-dots. The high resolution scan of C1s region (Fig. 5b) can be deconvoluted into four peaks with the binding energies of 283.99, 284.82, 285.80 and 288.21 eV, attributed to CC, CO/CS, CN, and CO species, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 5c) mainly consists of two peaks centering at 400.09 and 402.39 eV, respectively, ascribing to pyrrolic N/pyridine and pyridine-N-oxide [36]. For the high-resolution S2p region (Fig. 5d), the peak can be fitted into two distinct components at 163.23 and 164.31 eV, which are assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 sites of –CS covalent bond of the thiophene-like S [14,17].

In order to assess the stability of CL-NAC+CA dots for bearing circumstances, various experiments were performed. Fig. 6 a displays the pH effect on the emission intensity of CL-NAC+CA dots. As the pH augmenting from 2 to 13, the relationship between the PL intensity and pH fails to show any regularity. The presence of many OH, SH, NH and COO− groups on CL-NAC+CA dots may be responsible for this relationship, which can be explained by the amphoteric properties similar to amino acids [37]. This irregular pH dependence is further supported by zeta potential analysis (Fig. A.8). Interestingly, CL-NAC+CA dots can also dissolve in various types of organic solvents (see Fig. A.9). As shown in Fig. 6b, the emission behavior of CL-NAC+CA dots is independent of ionic strength of the medium. Its fluorescence intensity remains constant with varied concentrations of NaCl in the aqueous solution. The photo-stability test of CL-NAC+CA dots was performed at an extremely low concentration (abs = 0.01) in ultrapure water irradiated by a xenon lamp. The fluorescence decreased only 7% of the original emission within 120 min (Fig. 6c), exhibiting highly resistance to photo-bleaching. The PL thermal stability performance of C-dots was also measured.

Fig. 6d illustrates the temperature-dependent emission of CL-NAC+CA dots. It is clear that the emission intensity declined linearly when temperature increases. This is the result of thermal activation of nonradiative channels (such as trapping by surface/defect/ionized impurity states) which could be expressed by τNR = τ0 exp(Ea/KBT), where τNR is nonradiative lifetime, Ea is the activation energy, and KB is the Boltzmann constant. It is clear that when the temperature (T) goes up, the nonradiative lifetime (τNR) declined. While the quantum efficiency and fluorescence intensity can be expressed as η = (1 + τR/τNR)−1, and I(T) = I0/[1+(τR/τ0)] × exp(-Ea/KBT)], respectively, where τR represents radiative lifetime. Thereby, the enhancement of temperature (T) led to the decrease in quantum efficiency and fluorescence intensity [38]. It is worth mentioning that the fluorescence totally recovered when the temperature falls back to 30 °C.

As exhibited in Fig. 7 , the CL-NAC+CA dots in aqueous solution possess one shoulder absorption peak at 239 nm and one main absorption peak at 345 nm, respectively. The peak at 239 nm ascribes to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. The other transition at 345 nm results in much enhanced emission, owing to the n-π* transition, related to both doping and surface functionalization at the edge of carbon core [31]. Strong blue luminescence under the illumination of UV (365 nm) light of the CL-NAC+CA dots aqueous solution can be clearly observed (inset of Fig. 7). The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of the CL-NAC+CA dots aqueous solution are 345 and 427 nm, respectively. The apparent UV–vis absorption band at 345 nm corresponds well to the excitation spectra.

The PL spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots in water solution are illustrated in 3D-measurements (Fig. 8 ). Emission spectra were acquired at different excitation wavelengths (λ ex) with 10 nm increments from 200 nm to 460 nm, while the excitation spectra were obtained at different emission wavelengths (λ em) with 10 nm increments from 350 nm to 600 nm. The PL emission peaks are located at ca. 427 nm with the λ ex at 239 nm and 345 nm. When increasing the excitation wavelength from 200 to 380 nm, the emission maxima of CL-NAC+CA dots maintain at the same position, and the emission intensity changes in accord with the variation trend of the absorption intensity (Fig. 7). The organic fluorophores in CA-derived C-dots, which show no excitation dependent emission, are expected to contribute to the claimed “excitation independence” [15,25,31]. Whereas, further increasing excitation wavelength from 380 nm (the tail of the n-π* absorption peak) to 430 nm, the fluorescence peak obviously red shifts from 435 nm to 495 nm along with sharply decrease of PL intensity. This behavior of low intensity and red-shift of emission is often associated with surface- or defect states in the amorphous carbon shell of C-dots [31]. As different energy levels associated with different ‘‘surface states’’ [26], the very weak observed excitation-dependent emission over 380–430 nm indicates a relatively uniform and well-passivated C-dots surface. The excitation-independent PL behaviors of the product before suffering the carbonization stage were also studied. When the excitation wavelength changes from 200 nm to 420 nm, the corresponding PL emission peak maintains at ca. 447 nm without red-shifting (Fig. A.10). This is because in the polymerization stage, only polymer cluster were produced, while further elevating reaction temperature, carbon core along with the surface state caused by the introducing of N and S in C-dots surfaces were then formed.

To further investigate the origin of the C-dots fluorescence, the Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique was utilized to probe the fluorescence lifetime of CL-NAC+CA dots with different n(L-NAC)/5(CA) values (1, 4, 12), S-C-dots (C3-MPA+CA dots), N-C-dots (CL-Iso+CA dots) and another type of N,S-C-dots (CL-Met+CA dots), the results are exhibited and compared in Fig. 9 and Table 2 , respectively. The fluorescence decay curves of all C-dots samples can be fitted using biexponential functions I(t) based on a non-linear least squares analysis using the following equation:
(1)
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
(
− t
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
(
− t
τ 2
)
where τ1 and τ2 represent the decay time constants, A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved decay lifetime of τ1 and τ2 [39]. The average lifetime (τavg) of C-dots was calculated using equation:
(2)
τ
avg
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A
1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The decay fitting results are listed in Table 2, with a short lifetime τ1 and a long lifetime τ2 for each sample. The biexponential behavior of lifetime suggests that there exist two different emissive sites (fast and slow decay components) [40], corresponding to the recombination of surface state and molecular state, respectively. Compared to molecular state, the emission originated from surface state generally exhibits a shorter recombination lifetime. It is thus speculated that the short lifetime (τ1) of ∼1.0 ns may be attributed to surface state in C-dots surfaces, while the long lifetime (τ2) may be ascribed to molecular state.

In the case of the PL decay of CL-NAC+CA dots with different n(L-NAC)/5(CA) values (1, 4, 12), with the increase of n(L-NAC)/5(CA) values, τ1 does not significantly change, while τ2 is gradually increased, indicating the lifetime change is mainly the result of the changing molecular state of C-dots. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetimes of different kinds of C-dots were investigated to disclose the PL mechanism. In Table 2, the τ2 proportions of N,S-C-dots (CL-NAC+CA dots) (99.14%) and N-C-dots (97.94%) are much higher than that of S-C-dots (76.76%), suggesting that τ2 is related to CN, dominating the fluorescence properties of C-dots [41]. Furthermore, we also studied the fluorescence lifetime of CL-Met+CA dots, the QY of which is much low. As shown in Table 2, the value of τ2 of CL-Met+CA dots (5.25 ns) providing 91.04% is quite close to that of S-C-dots (5.1 ns), much shorter than the τ2 of N-C-dots (8.53 ns) and N,S-C-dots (CL-NAC+CA dots, 9.97 ns). This further supported our declaration that different structures of N,S-precursors can significantly affect the optical properties of the resulting C-dots. The average fluorescence lifetime of the tested samples was then further calculated, where the lifetime of N,S-C-dots (CL-NAC+CA dots) and N-C-dots is longer than that of S-C-dots. It is worth noting that these average PL lifetimes, including all the lifetime components, only provide the information of relative emission strength, which could not correlate with PL QY precisely [16].

In summary, we have facially synthesized N,S-C-dots with a high QY of 55.7% by thermal treatment of CA as carbon source and L-NAC as N and S dopant. The experimental parameters have been systematically optimized and the structure has been carefully characterized. Specially, we have carefully investigated the origins of PL emission of C-dots. Our results imply that molecular fluorophores are important building blocks in CA-derived C-dots. Compared with surface state, the molecular state induced by the fluorophores covalently bonded onto C-dots surfaces dominating the fluorescence properties of these CA-derived C-dots. More importantly, the PL performances of various types of CA-derived N,S-C-dots, N-C-dots and S-C-dots have been comprehensively compared. And for the first time, the efficient chemical structure motif of reactant is reported, which could from small fluorophore molecules with high PL during the synthesis, favorable to PL performances of resulting C-dots. It turned out that the chemical structure motif RHN–CCSH govern the molecular state of the CA-derived C-dots, and thus dominating PL properties of CA-derived C-dots. Because the molecular state of CA-derived C-dots is highly fluorescent, more similar rationally designed synthetic approaches could be developed to fabricate these C-dots with high stability and emission in the visible region.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2016CFB615), the Fundamental Research Funds for the “Central Universities”, South-central University for Nationalities (CZY15020, CZW15017) and South-central University for Nationalities Graduate Student Outstanding Thesis Cultivation Project (2015xwlwpy007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendices
ce:section.ce:para: The appendices consist of the PL spectra and UV–vis spectra of CL-NAC+CA dots prepared at 70 °C for various reaction times (3, 6, 9, and 12 h) without carbonalization, PL spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra of a series of S-C-dots, N-C-dots and N,S-C-dots, PL spectra and UV–visible absorption spectra of CL-Cys+CA dots and CL-NAC+CA dots (10 mg mL−1) prepared at 70 °C for 12 h without carbonalization, the proposed structure of polymer cluster, fluorescence chromatogram of the product 3 generated by L-NAC and CA in the carbonization stage, TEM image of the CL-NAC+CA dots, Zeta potential changes in the different pH, photographs of CL-NAC+CA dots dissolved in various types of organic solvents under UV light of 365 nm, the excitation-independent PL behaviors of CL-NAC+CA dots prepared at 70 °C for 12 h without carbonalization, and fitting parameters of the corresponding PL decay curve.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2016.10.089.
